insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The analysis sub-package (adaptive.ts, review.ts, spin.ts, stuck.ts) represents a rich state-machine for detecting and recovering from agent stalls, which is a mature pattern for long-running autonomous loops.
Zero coupling to any zone outside its own package boundary means hench changes cannot accidentally break sourcevision or web — a strong safety property for an autonomous agent.
Perfect cohesion at 158 files is a strong signal that the gateway pattern is correctly concentrating all cross-package imports, keeping internal files free of ad-hoc cross-package dependencies.

The agent analysis layer (adaptive, review, spin, stuck) suggests well-decomposed recovery logic; ensure each module has unit tests covering the stuck-detection and recovery transitions that are hardest to exercise in integration.
Run history is stored in .hench/runs/ outside this zone — confirm that the hench package treats these as opaque output artifacts and never imports from them, preserving the one-way data flow.
Zone "autonomous-agent" has files across 32 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

2716 internal calls with zero outgoing confirms the gateway pattern creates a true black-box boundary: all internal complexity (agent loops, analysis, tool dispatch) is fully contained and the only escape hatch is the explicit rex-gateway re-export surface.

Call-graph black-box pattern: 2716 internal edges, 0 outgoing. The gateway pattern scales to large packages without leakage — this zone is an exemplar for how to isolate a complex execution layer from its domain dependencies.

The stuck-detection and recovery layer (adaptive.ts, review.ts, spin.ts, stuck.ts) is fully internal with no interface hook exposed through rex-gateway. If recovery heuristics (e.g. spin thresholds, review triggers) need to be tuned per-project, there is no extension point short of forking the analysis internals.

With 2716 internal call-graph edges and zero outgoing, the autonomous-agent zone is a true black box — but this also means observability tooling (tracing, metrics) can only be injected at the zone entry point (hench CLI), not at sub-layer boundaries inside the agent loop.

The agent recovery sub-layer (adaptive/review/spin/stuck) exposes no tunable interface through the rex-gateway or any public hook. Project-specific recovery thresholds are hardcoded inside the black box, making per-project behavioural customisation impossible without modifying package internals.

The zone ID 'autonomous-agent' does not match the package name 'hench' — every other package-level zone uses the package name as its identity anchor (rex-runtime-state, web-build-tooling, web-viewer). This naming divergence means zone-based tooling queries and package-based tooling queries use different identifiers for the same code, requiring a mental mapping that will cause errors in automated zone-pin rules referencing the package.

Zone ID 'autonomous-agent' diverges from package name 'hench', unlike all other package zones which anchor their IDs to the package name. Renaming to 'hench-agent' or 'hench' would align zone ID with package identity and eliminate the identifier mismatch in zone-pin rules and tooling queries.

The deleted file shown in git status is tests/cli-contract.test.mjs (root tests/ directory) while the zone manifest lists tests/e2e/cli-contract.test.mjs (tests/e2e/ subdirectory) — these are distinct paths. The existing insight conflates them; the e2e-subdirectory version may still exist, making the stale-classification concern either moot or different from what was previously described.

The 414 internal call-graph edges with zero outgoing represent test-to-test and test-to-fixture calls only. The absence of any outgoing edges means that when a CLI entry point changes its contract, no static analysis signal propagates to this zone — breakage is only detectable at runtime, not at graph-analysis time.

The previously noted stale-file finding cites tests/cli-contract.test.mjs (root) as deleted while the zone contains tests/e2e/cli-contract.test.mjs (subdirectory). If these are distinct files, the zone manifest may be accurate and the stale-classification observation is a false alarm. A path-exact audit is required before acting on the deletion finding.

The suite mixes two structurally distinct test categories: short-lived command tests (init, status, next, config) that complete in milliseconds and long-running server process tests (cli-dev, cli-web/start) that must spawn and kill background processes. These two categories have different flakiness profiles — server tests risk port conflicts and timing races in parallel runs — but they are not separated or annotated in any way within the zone.

The absence of TypeScript (.ts) across all 19 e2e test files while all production code is TypeScript creates a type-safety gap: e2e tests cannot catch type errors introduced in production CLI interfaces and will only fail at runtime when an API contract breaks. The gap is widest for architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js, which enforce structural rules but cannot express typed expectations about the import shapes they inspect.

Server-lifecycle e2e tests (cli-dev.test.js, any test that spawns a background server) should be tagged or placed in a subdirectory (e.g. tests/e2e/server/) separate from command e2e tests. This allows parallel execution of the command subset without port-conflict risk and makes flaky test isolation faster. The current flat structure hides this execution-model distinction.

All 19 e2e test files use .js (or .mjs) rather than .ts. Migrating to .ts would close the type-safety gap between tests and production CLIs, allowing TypeScript to catch contract breaks before runtime. At minimum, architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js — which enforce typed structural rules — are the highest-value candidates for TypeScript migration.

The bidirectional call-graph relationship between packages-rex:unit-analyze and packages-rex:cli (239 calls outgoing, 100 calls incoming) mirrors the web↔web-viewer bidirectional pattern at the inter-package level — suggesting bidirectional coupling is a recurring structural smell in this codebase, not an isolated web-package anomaly.

packages-rex:unit-analyze ↔ packages-rex:cli shows 239+100 bidirectional call-graph edges within the rex package. This internal bidirectional coupling mirrors the web↔web-viewer pattern and risks the same logical entanglement: changes to cli layer can ripple into unit-analyze and vice versa with no clear dependency direction.

The rotated log file uses suffix-numeric naming (.jsonl → .1.jsonl), implying an unbounded rotation scheme: as rotation events accumulate, files .1.jsonl, .2.jsonl, .3.jsonl will appear with no documented maximum count or total size cap. Without a retention policy enforced by the rex package, the .rex/ directory will grow unboundedly during long-running projects.

The zone contains both machine-written files (prd.json, execution-log.jsonl, archive.json) and one human-readable file (workflow.md). This mixed authorship means the zone has two different consistency mechanisms: prd.json is the canonical source of truth enforced by code, while workflow.md is a derived human-readable view with no enforced regeneration trigger. A stale workflow.md is indistinguishable from a current one without diffing against prd.json.

The suffix-numeric log rotation scheme (.1.jsonl, .2.jsonl) has no documented or enforced upper bound. Add a MAX_LOG_SEGMENTS constant to the rex package and enforce cleanup of segments beyond the limit during every rotation event to prevent unbounded .rex/ directory growth in long-running projects.

workflow.md has no enforced regeneration trigger — it is the only human-readable file in a zone of machine-written artifacts, and its staleness is silently indistinguishable. Either (a) regenerate workflow.md on every rex write operation or (b) delete workflow.md and link users to 'rex status' for the human-readable view, eliminating the dual-source risk entirely.

These misclassifications reduce the reliability of archetype-based tooling (entrypoint audits, gateway surface enumeration) across the web package.

concurrent-execution-metrics.ts in web-dashboard and the analytics files in usage-analytics-service represent split analytics concerns at the zone level — the same design smell at a smaller scale as crash-recovery's split from web-dashboard. Establishing a policy that all server-side metrics files belong in usage-analytics-service would close this gap without requiring a new zone.

The [gateway] archetype is used for two structurally distinct roles in the web package: cross-package import isolation (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) and intra-zone public interface (task-usage.ts). A second archetype (e.g. [zone-api] or [facade]) would disambiguate these roles and restore the gateway archetype's value as a cross-package dependency audit tool.

Across both test-only zones, zero incoming edges exist from any other zone — confirming that no production code imports from test files. This invariant holds across all 10 visible zones simultaneously and represents a global architectural invariant worth encoding as a CI-enforced policy rule.

The two legitimate script files in developer-utilities (hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs, test-zone-consistency.mjs) are both .mjs modules at the repository root level. They have more in common with the monorepo-orchestration zone's cli.js/web.js/ci.js entry-point pattern than with either the web viewer tests or each other — a targeted zone-pin moving them into monorepo-orchestration or a dedicated scripts zone would be more semantically accurate than the current placement.

No zone in this batch or previous batches has an explicit coupling-measurement-method annotation distinguishing import-graph coupling from subprocess coupling. Subprocess-coupled zones (cli-e2e-tests, monorepo-orchestration) report coupling 0 — identical to genuinely decoupled zones (developer-utilities, web-landing). Without this annotation, automated health scoring cannot distinguish intentional subprocess isolation from accidental disconnection, producing false-equivalent health grades across structurally different zone types.

packages-sourcevision:analyzers-3 → packages-sourcevision:unit has 570 call-graph edges — the highest single inter-zone call count in the codebase. A single analyzers sub-zone accounting for this call volume is a god-module signal; decomposing it into focused single-responsibility analyzers would reduce blast radius when any one analyzer changes.

Bidirectional coupling appears at three independent scales (inter-package, intra-package, zone-level) with no documented architectural policy against it. Without an explicit dependency-direction convention enforced in CI (e.g. via a dependency-cruiser rule), new bidirectional edges will continue to be introduced undetected.

Zone classification drift is present in at least 3 zones (cli-e2e-tests, developer-utilities, web-build-tooling) simultaneously. The current re-analysis cadence is insufficient to keep zone membership synchronized with the working tree; a pre-commit or CI hook that fails on mismatched zone membership would close this gap.

Zone dissolution (crash-recovery → web-dashboard) has been executed without adding a sub-zone configuration pin to preserve the semantic boundary of the crash detection/recovery feature. The project has a pattern of dissolving zones to fix metric violations, but no established pattern for using sub-zone pins to maintain semantic boundaries post-dissolution. Without a pin policy, dissolved zones will silently diffuse into hub zones over time.

The [gateway] archetype is overloaded to mean both 'cross-package import aggregation point' (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) and 'intra-zone public API' (task-usage.ts). This ambiguity makes it impossible to enumerate the cross-package gateway surface by archetype query alone, undermining the auditability guarantee the gateway pattern is designed to provide.

Zone cohesion score of 1 is achieved by two structurally different mechanisms across the visible zones: (1) dense mutual imports (cli-e2e-tests, hench) and (2) complete graph isolation (developer-utilities, web-landing). Health dashboards that surface cohesion scores without distinguishing these mechanisms will assign identical 'healthy' grades to intentionally cohesive zones and entropy-sink zones, hiding classification drift behind a green metric.

scripts/ directory files (hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs, test-zone-consistency.mjs) are currently absorbed into developer-utilities by graph isolation rather than belonging to any intentional zone. As the scripts/ directory grows, each new isolated script will land here by default, making developer-utilities a permanent entropy sink unless zone pins are used to enforce explicit membership for all scripts/ files.

The codebase has exactly one zone with dual-metric degradation (cohesion < 0.75 AND coupling > 0): web-build-tooling. All other zones fail at most one metric. This makes web-build-tooling categorically different from every other zone — not just a zone with a naming problem, but the only zone where the two primary structural health signals simultaneously indicate fragility.

Zone naming conventions are inconsistent across the three package-level zones: rex-runtime-state uses the package name as a prefix (rex-*), web-build-tooling uses a functional descriptor (web-*), and autonomous-agent uses a semantic role name with no package-name anchor. A uniform convention of '<package-name>-<role>' (hench-agent, rex-state, web-build) would make zone IDs self-documenting and align them with the package identity used everywhere else in the toolchain.

The codebase has two classes of invisible state dependency that bypass both the gateway pattern and import-graph analysis: (1) subprocess CLI contracts in monorepo-orchestration and (2) config file format contracts between config.js and domain packages. Neither class has a CI-enforced contract test. Adding targeted contract tests for both — CLI flag smoke tests and config schema validation — would close the two largest static-analysis blind spots simultaneously.

Three zones (developer-docs, monorepo-orchestration, rex-runtime-state) achieve cohesion 1.0 for structurally different reasons: developer-docs has no code imports at all (graph isolation), monorepo-orchestration uses subprocess spawn (import-invisible coupling), and rex-runtime-state consists of pure data artifacts (no import surface). Treating all three as equivalent 'healthy cohesion' zones obscures that only rex-runtime-state has genuinely artifact-appropriate cohesion — the other two are measurement artifacts of the import-graph approach.

The aggregation-cache.ts fingerprinting mechanism creates silent cross-domain path coupling to all three domain directories (hench, rex, sourcevision) via hardcoded string construction. This is a second class of import-graph-invisible coupling alongside subprocess invocation — 'filesystem-path coupling' — that requires a dedicated audit strategy separate from import-graph or call-graph analysis.

mcp-deps.ts (deprecated super-gateway), aggregation-cache.ts (filesystem path coupling), and usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts (injected ID collector) are three distinct workarounds for the same underlying constraint: web cannot take direct runtime dependencies on hench. Each workaround uses a different mechanism (re-export shim, stat() calls, function injection) with no shared convention. Documenting these as canonical patterns for cross-tier data access would prevent ad-hoc workarounds from accumulating further.

concurrent-execution-metrics.ts is the only file in the codebase confirmed to be a deliberate parallel implementation of a hench class. The pattern — reimplement hench logic in web rather than import it — is correct given the four-tier hierarchy, but has no discoverability mechanism. A convention such as a JSDoc @mirrorOf tag referencing the canonical hench source would make drift between the two implementations detectable.

The two test-only zones exhibit the most extreme example of 'cohesion score inversion' in the codebase: developer-utilities has cohesion 1 with 4 files and 138 edges (artifact of misclassification inflating density), while cli-e2e-tests has cohesion 1 with 19 files and 414 edges (genuine dense coupling). Both score identically in automated health summaries despite one being a classification error and the other being well-structured. This is the clearest demonstration in the codebase that cohesion score alone is not a reliable zone health signal without a cohesion-mechanism annotation.

The codebase now has four documented categories of invisible coupling — subprocess spawning (cli-e2e-tests, monorepo-orchestration), shared mutable file state (rex-runtime-state via .rex/prd.json), runtime file-system reads (hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs → .sourcevision/callgraph.json), and runtime introspection (architecture-policy.test.js inspecting module graphs) — none of which are annotated, enumerated, or enforced in any automated way. A coupling-taxonomy document listing all four categories with their representative zones and detection gaps would make this invisible surface auditable.

Across both test zones, all test files use .js or .mjs while all production code is .ts. This is a consistent monorepo-level convention — tests are not typed. The convention is implicit and unenforceable: nothing prevents a future contributor from writing a typed test (.ts) or an untyped production module (.mjs). Documenting this as an explicit decision (and the tradeoff accepted) in CLAUDE.md or PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md would prevent confusion and enable lint-level enforcement of the convention.

Establish a zone-ID naming convention of '<package-name>-<role>' and apply it consistently: rename 'autonomous-agent' to 'hench-agent', enforce that all future zones follow the pattern. This eliminates the identifier mismatch between zone IDs and package names that currently requires a mental mapping table for zone-based tooling.

The two largest static-analysis blind spots — subprocess CLI contracts (monorepo-orchestration) and config file format contracts (config.js ↔ domain packages) — share the property that they are invisible to import-graph tooling yet represent real coupling. Add a single CI job that (a) smoke-tests key CLI flags for each entry point and (b) validates config file schemas against documented formats, closing both blind spots with one targeted test suite.

Three files in web-dashboard encode domain directory paths as hardcoded string literals (aggregation-cache.ts for all three domains, usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts for .hench/). Introduce path constant re-exports in each domain's public API so the web package can derive correct paths without string duplication — this makes path changes in rex or hench propagate to the web cache automatically.

mcp-deps.ts must be removed after confirming zero active callers. Its continued presence as a re-export shim for both cross-package gateways creates a discoverable but policy-violating import path that future maintainers may use by accident. The @deprecated tag is insufficient protection against accidental use.

Four structurally distinct invisible-coupling mechanisms have been identified (subprocess, shared file state, file-system reads, runtime introspection) across multiple zones, with zero annotation or enforcement for any of them. Create a COUPLING_TAXONOMY.md or add a section to PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md enumerating each mechanism, its representative zones, and the recommended mitigation (contract tests, schema versioning, README annotation). This closes the documentation gap that makes invisible coupling silently accumulate.

The implicit convention of .js/.mjs for tests and .ts for production code is monorepo-wide but undocumented and unenforced. Add an explicit statement to PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and a lint rule (e.g. a no-.ts-in-tests or no-.js-in-src glob pattern in vitest/eslint config) to make the convention machine-verifiable. Without enforcement, the convention will erode as the codebase grows.

Perfect cohesion at 158 files is a strong signal that the gateway pattern is correctly concentrating all cross-package imports, keeping internal files free of ad-hoc cross-package dependencies.
Run history is stored in .hench/runs/ outside this zone — confirm that the hench package treats these as opaque output artifacts and never imports from them, preserving the one-way data flow.

The agent analysis layer (adaptive, review, spin, stuck) suggests well-decomposed recovery logic; ensure each module has unit tests covering the stuck-detection and recovery transitions that are hardest to exercise in integration.
Having architecture-policy and domain-isolation tests co-located with behavioral CLI tests is an excellent practice — it makes architectural drift visible in the same CI run that validates behavior.
The zone is purely test files with cohesion 1 and coupling 0, confirming correct isolation — no production source is accidentally mixed in.

cli-contract.test.mjs uses an .mjs extension while all 16 sibling test files use .js. This inconsistency can cause the file to be missed or handled differently by vitest glob patterns — normalize to .js or .ts.
No CHANGELOG or CONTRIBUTING guide is detected in this zone; given the multi-package monorepo structure, a top-level CONTRIBUTING.md would lower onboarding friction.
The 2026-03-03 dated analysis file mixes archival notes with living design docs; consider a docs/archive/ subdirectory to distinguish reference material from active design documents.

prd-steward-vision.md and level-system-reference.md suggest a planned 'level' concept for the PRD hierarchy; if implemented, cross-referencing these docs from the relevant source files would improve traceability.
Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0 confirm this zone is correctly scoped: it owns orchestration concerns and nothing else leaks in or out.
No test files are detected in this zone; orchestration scripts that spawn subprocesses should have at least smoke-level integration tests to catch CLI contract regressions.
The presence of config.js alongside cli.js and web.js reflects the unified config command pattern described in CLAUDE.md — all three are peer entry points, not nested utilities.
acknowledged-findings.json and pending-proposals.json represent workflow-phase state; if these accumulate indefinitely they may grow stale — a periodic pruning strategy would reduce noise.
archive.json alongside prd.json implies a soft-delete pattern for removed PRD items, which is good for auditability and recovery.
workflow.md is human-readable state that duplicates information from prd.json; ensure it is always regenerated from the canonical JSON source to avoid drift.
At 5 files this zone is small but cohesive (0.75); if analytics grows (e.g. cost breakdowns, per-model tracking), it has a clear home and won't scatter across the larger dashboard zone.

The gateway pattern is correctly applied: task-usage.ts re-exports the public API while incremental-task-usage.ts and usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts remain internal — this prevents callers from bypassing the stable interface.
usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts suggests background work is happening inside the web process; verify this is intentional and not better placed in a dedicated worker or cron mechanism.

Developer zone hints explicitly identify elapsed-time.ts, use-tick.ts, lazy-children.ts, listener-lifecycle.ts, and task-audit.ts as misclassified viewer components; relocating them to the web-viewer zone would raise cohesion and eliminate the bidirectional dependency cycle.

The mutual import cycle between this zone and web-viewer (10 + 7 cross-zone imports) is a structural smell; after viewer file relocation, imports should flow one-way from build tooling into the viewer, not back out.
build.js and dev.js are correctly classified as entrypoints; they should remain in this zone as the canonical build entry surface, separate from the runtime viewer application code.
Cohesion of 0.98 with only 0.02 coupling is exceptional for a 357-file zone — the community detection algorithm found a very tight, self-contained cluster.
Gateway modules (domain-gateway.ts, rex-gateway.ts) are correctly co-located inside the zone rather than floating at a package boundary, keeping the cross-package import surface auditable.
Zone size (357 files) means it carries the entire web package surface; if sub-zones are not introduced, future refactors will have no algorithmic boundary to anchor on.
Zero coupling with any other zone means the landing page can be developed or replaced without risk of cascading changes.

check-gateway-regex.mjs and check-gateway-test.mjs are governance/CI scripts co-located with landing page assets purely by graph isolation — they would be better placed in a dev-tooling or scripts zone.

landing.ts classified as a service rather than an entrypoint may indicate the file has mixed responsibilities (bootstrapping + logic); confirm it delegates to imported modules rather than doing work inline.
PNG assets correctly appear in the import graph as zone members rather than being silently excluded — this confirms sourcevision tracks binary static assets alongside source files.

The zone has no entry points, meaning nothing in the codebase statically imports these files; they are referenced at runtime via HTML or CSS, which is correct but means static analysis cannot trace their usage.
Two logo variants (darkmode/lightmode) suggest a theme-switching feature; confirm the viewer's theme logic references both and neither is stale.

Call-graph black-box pattern: 2716 internal edges, 0 outgoing. The gateway pattern scales to large packages without leakage — this zone is an exemplar for how to isolate a complex execution layer from its domain dependencies.

E2E tests couple to production CLI packages exclusively through subprocess spawning, not imports. Import-graph coupling metrics are structurally blind to this dependency class — treat coupling 0 as 'not measurable' rather than 'no coupling' for this zone.
cli-contract.test.mjs is present in the zone manifest but deleted in the working tree. The zone will silently misreport file count and cohesion until re-analyzed.

Orchestration scripts communicate exclusively via subprocess spawn, not imports — this means CLI contract regressions (renamed flags, changed exit codes) are undetectable by static import-graph analysis and require dedicated CLI contract tests to enforce.

At 357 files and 0.81% external call rate, web-dashboard functions as a monolithic hub. The star topology it anchors is architecturally valid but means any zone decomposition must be done by sub-zone analysis within web-dashboard, not by extracting peer zones from outside it.

Perfectly isolated files (no imports in or out) always achieve cohesion: 1 by definition regardless of semantic relatedness. web-landing's perfect cohesion score is a metric artifact caused by the two governance scripts, not evidence of a genuinely cohesive zone.

Binary and HTML assets that are referenced at runtime via HTML/CSS rather than JS imports create a governance blind spot: static analysis cannot verify liveness, duplication, or correct theming logic. Supplement import-graph analysis with an HTML asset-reference scan to close this gap.

The unidirectional coupling from web-dashboard into usage-analytics-service (not the reverse) confirms correct dependency direction for a consumed service. This is the only zone in the web package batch with both correct directionality and a gateway enforcing the public interface.

web-build-tooling has 32 incoming runtime call-graph edges — disproportionate for a build-infrastructure zone. This is caused by misclassified viewer components that are imported by web-viewer at runtime. Relocating them would drop incoming coupling to near zero.

The agent recovery sub-layer (adaptive/review/spin/stuck) exposes no tunable interface through the rex-gateway or any public hook. Project-specific recovery thresholds are hardcoded inside the black box, making per-project behavioural customisation impossible without modifying package internals.

The previously noted stale-file finding cites tests/cli-contract.test.mjs (root) as deleted while the zone contains tests/e2e/cli-contract.test.mjs (subdirectory). If these are distinct files, the zone manifest may be accurate and the stale-classification observation is a false alarm. A path-exact audit is required before acting on the deletion finding.

Config reading is implemented independently in each domain package (rex reads .rex/config.json, hench reads .hench/config.json, sourcevision reads .sourcevision/) while config.js at the orchestration layer provides a unified view. There is no shared config-reader utility in the Foundation tier, so format changes must be patched in multiple independent implementations.

packages-rex:unit-analyze ↔ packages-rex:cli shows 239+100 bidirectional call-graph edges within the rex package. This internal bidirectional coupling mirrors the web↔web-viewer pattern and risks the same logical entanglement: changes to cli layer can ripple into unit-analyze and vice versa with no clear dependency direction.

task-usage.ts uses the [gateway] archetype to denote an intra-zone public interface, but the project's gateway pattern (CLAUDE.md) defines gateways specifically as cross-package import aggregation points. Overloading the archetype for both roles makes it impossible to enumerate cross-package gateways by archetype alone without false positives.

32 incoming runtime call-graph edges into a zone classified as build infrastructure means production code has a hidden runtime dependency on files that appear to be dev-only tooling. Any optimization pass that treats build-tooling as non-runtime (tree-shaking, conditional bundling) would silently drop live UI components.

Zone name 'web-build-tooling' is semantically incorrect for its current membership: it contains 5 production runtime viewer components (elapsed-time.ts, use-tick.ts, lazy-children.ts, listener-lifecycle.ts, task-audit.ts) alongside build scripts. The misleading name is an active source of zone classification drift and should be resolved by relocating the viewer files.

mcp-deps.ts is classified [utility] with no declared gateway role, but its name implies it aggregates MCP dependency imports. If it re-imports from rex-gateway.ts or domain-gateway.ts at runtime, it creates an implicit second aggregation layer inside the hub zone that bypasses the explicit gateway pattern and makes the cross-package import surface harder to audit.

packages/web/src/server/concurrent-execution-metrics.ts tracks server-level execution metrics but resides in web-dashboard rather than usage-analytics-service. Splitting analytics concerns between the hub zone and the dedicated analytics zone makes it harder to audit the full analytics surface and sets a precedent for future metrics landing in the hub by default.

landing.ts archetype is [service] rather than [entrypoint]. If this file bootstraps the landing page (as its name and role suggest), the misclassification causes it to be excluded from entrypoint-based analysis, dead-code detection, and bundle entry audits that rely on the archetype field to identify root modules.

Zone ID 'autonomous-agent' diverges from package name 'hench', unlike all other package zones which anchor their IDs to the package name. Renaming to 'hench-agent' or 'hench' would align zone ID with package identity and eliminate the identifier mismatch in zone-pin rules and tooling queries.

All 19 e2e test files use .js (or .mjs) rather than .ts. Migrating to .ts would close the type-safety gap between tests and production CLIs, allowing TypeScript to catch contract breaks before runtime. At minimum, architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js — which enforce typed structural rules — are the highest-value candidates for TypeScript migration.

Server-lifecycle e2e tests (cli-dev.test.js, any test that spawns a background server) should be tagged or placed in a subdirectory (e.g. tests/e2e/server/) separate from command e2e tests. This allows parallel execution of the command subset without port-conflict risk and makes flaky test isolation faster. The current flat structure hides this execution-model distinction.

No mechanism exists to detect drift between hand-authored docs (command flags, MCP tool names, config keys) and actual package behavior. Add a docs-validation step to CI that spot-checks at least the command reference sections of README.md and CLAUDE.md against the live CLI help output to catch description drift before it misleads contributors.

Prescriptive design documents (prd-steward-vision.md, level-system-reference.md) and retrospective analysis documents (memory-*.md, dated analysis files) are co-located in a flat docs/ directory. Separate them into docs/design/ and docs/analysis/ subdirectories to make the navigational intent of each file immediately clear to new contributors.

The zone mixes slow-changing metadata files (.gitignore, .npmrc, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) with frequently-modified entry points (cli.js, web.js, config.js). Consider adding a zone-pin subdividing this zone into 'monorepo-root-config' (metadata) and 'monorepo-entry-points' (scripts) to expose change-rate differences and reduce blast-radius confusion during entry-point refactors.

config.js has an implicit schema dependency on domain config file formats that is undetectable by import-graph analysis and unprotected by the gateway pattern. Document the config file schemas as stable interfaces in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and add a schema-validation test in the CI pipeline to catch format drift before it silently breaks config.js.

The suffix-numeric log rotation scheme (.1.jsonl, .2.jsonl) has no documented or enforced upper bound. Add a MAX_LOG_SEGMENTS constant to the rex package and enforce cleanup of segments beyond the limit during every rotation event to prevent unbounded .rex/ directory growth in long-running projects.

workflow.md has no enforced regeneration trigger — it is the only human-readable file in a zone of machine-written artifacts, and its staleness is silently indistinguishable. Either (a) regenerate workflow.md on every rex write operation or (b) delete workflow.md and link users to 'rex status' for the human-readable view, eliminating the dual-source risk entirely.

The CollectAllIdsFn injection in usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts is documented as a zone-cycle workaround, not a generic pattern (see line 157-160 comment). Document this explicitly at the call site in the server startup code so the architectural context survives refactoring — otherwise a future cleanup may remove the injection without realizing it would reintroduce a zone cycle.

usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts writes audit output to .hench/usage-cleanup.jsonl, coupling the web server's data output path to hench's directory layout. The path should be configurable via the server context (ctx.henchDir or similar) rather than hardcoded, consistent with how rexDir is already injected.

After relocating misclassified viewer components, rename the zone from 'web-build-tooling' to 'web-build-scripts' or 'web-dev-infrastructure' to eliminate the semantic mismatch between the zone name and its actual runtime-vs-buildtime membership — the current name will continue to attract misclassification drift from developers who see 'build tooling' and place viewer build-time utilities here.

web-build-tooling is the only zone with BOTH cohesion < 0.75 (0.59) AND coupling > 0 (0.41) — the dual-metric failure pattern that indicates highest fragility. Relocating the five misclassified viewer components is a single action that should restore both metrics simultaneously. Treat as a priority fix above other zone health issues.

aggregation-cache.ts encodes domain directory paths (.hench/runs, .rex/execution-log.jsonl, .sourcevision/manifest.json) as string literals instead of importing path constants from rex and sourcevision packages. If either domain package relocates its data files, the aggregation cache will silently use stale paths with no compile-time error. Inject the paths as constructor parameters or derive them from shared constants.

concurrent-execution-metrics.ts duplicates hench's ConcurrentExecutionMetrics to avoid a cross-tier import — a correct but undocumented convention. Add a comment in both files (web and hench) cross-referencing the sibling implementation and stating the reason for duplication, so future maintainers don't consolidate them through an architecture-violating import.

mcp-deps.ts is @deprecated but still exports from both rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts, creating a third enumerable aggregation point that violates the one-gateway-per-source-package rule. Audit callers and remove the file once all import sites are migrated — the deprecation notice is not enough if any active imports remain.

check-gateway-regex.mjs and check-gateway-test.mjs share the 'gateway' name prefix with production gateway modules, polluting filename-based gateway surface audits. Rename them to check-gateway-compliance-*.mjs or move them under a scripts/ directory to distinguish governance tooling from the modules they govern.
